You are working on the pinterest/ktlint repository.
The codebase is at version 0.49 (commit: cbee8223).

## Issue to Resolve

Repeat format step until no more errors are found which can be auto-corrected
The order in which rules are executed can be influences with `Rule.VisitorModifier`. Now the network or rule execution dependencies get more complex, it becomes difficult to find that single order of rules which always resolves all format errors in a single trip. This is confusing for users of kltint.

If format finds any lint violations in a file that can be autocorrected, it should automatically rerun format on that file before returning the lint violations of the last format run. To prevent that a set of rules cause an infinite rerun of the format, no more than  3 reruns will be executed before returning the results.

## Relevant Files

The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue:
- `CHANGELOG.md`
- `RELEASE_TESTING.MD`
- `documentation/snapshot/docs/rules/experimental.md`

101→        LOGGER.debug { "Starting with linting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'" }
102→

121→
122→    /**
123→     * Fix style violations in [code] for lint errors when possible. If [code] is passed as file reference then the '.editorconfig' files on
124→     * the path are taken into account. For each lint violation found, the [callback] is invoked.
125→     *
126→     * @throws KtLintParseException if text is not a valid Kotlin code
127→     * @throws KtLintRuleException in case of internal failure caused by a bug in rule implementation
128→     */
129→    public fun format(
130→        code: Code,
131→        callback: (LintError, Boolean) -> Unit = { _, _ -> },
132→    ): String {
133→        LOGGER.debug { "Starting with formatting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'" }
134→
135→        val hasUTF8BOM = code.content.startsWith(UTF8_BOM)
136→        val ruleExecutionContext = createRuleExecutionContext(this, code)
137→
138→        var tripped = false
139→        var mutated = false
140→        val errors = mutableSetOf<Pair<LintError, Boolean>>()
141→        val visitorProvider = VisitorProvider(ruleExecutionContext.ruleProviders)
142→        visitorProvider
143→            .visitor()
144→            .invoke { rule ->
145→                ruleExecutionContext.executeRule(rule, true) { offset, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected ->
146→                    tripped = true
147→                    if (canBeAutoCorrected) {
148→                        mutated = true
149→                        /**
150→                         * Rebuild the suppression locator after each change in the AST as the offsets of the
151→                         * suppression hints might have changed.
152→                         */
153→                        ruleExecutionContext.rebuildSuppressionLocator()
154→                    }
155→                    val (line, col) = ruleExecutionContext.positionInTextLocator(offset)
156→                    errors.add(
157→                        Pair(
158→                            LintError(line, col, rule.ruleId, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected),
159→                            // It is assumed that a rule that emits that an error can be autocorrected, also
160→                            // does correct the error.
161→                            canBeAutoCorrected,
162→                        ),
163→                    )
164→                    // In trace mode report the violation immediately. The order in which violations are actually found might be different
165→                    // from the order in which they are reported. For debugging purposes it cn be helpful to know the exact order in which
166→                    // violations are being solved.
167→                    LOGGER.trace {
168→                        "Format violation: ${code.fileNameOrStdin()}:$line:$col: $errorMessage (${rule.ruleId})" +
169→                            canBeAutoCorrected.ifFalse { " [cannot be autocorrected]" }
170→                    }
171→                }
172→            }
173→        if (tripped) {
174→            visitorProvider
175→                .visitor()
176→                .invoke { rule ->
177→                    ruleExecutionContext.executeRule(
178→                        rule,
179→                        false,
180→                    ) { offset, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected ->
181→                        val (line, col) = ruleExecutionContext.positionInTextLocator(offset)
182→                        errors.add(
183→                            Pair(
184→                                LintError(line, col, rule.ruleId, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected),
185→                                // It is assumed that a rule only corrects an error after it has emitted an
186→                                // error and indicating that it actually can be autocorrected.
187→                                false,
188→                            ),
189→                        )
190→                    }
191→                }
192→        }
193→
194→        errors
195→            .sortedWith { (l), (r) -> if (l.line != r.line) l.line - r.line else l.col - r.col }
196→            .forEach { (e, corrected) -> callback(e, corrected) }
197→
198→        if (!mutated) {
199→            return code.content
200→        }
201→
202→        val formattedCode =
203→            ruleExecutionContext
204→                .rootNode
205→                .text
206→                .replace("\n", ruleExecutionContext.determineLineSeparator(code.content))
207→        return if (hasUTF8BOM) {
208→            UTF8_BOM + formattedCode
209→        } else {
210→            formattedCode
211→        }.also {
212→            LOGGER.debug("Finished with formatting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'")
213→        }
214→    }
215→
216→    private fun RuleExecutionContext.determineLineSeparator(fileContent: String): String {
217→        val eol = editorConfig[END_OF_LINE_PROPERTY]
218→        return when {
219→            eol == crlf || eol != lf && fileContent.lastIndexOf('\r') != -1 -> "\r\n"
220→            else -> "\n"
221→        }
222→    }
223→
224→    /**
225→     * Generates Kotlin `.editorconfig` file section content based on a path to a file or directory. Given that path, all '.editorconfig'
226→     * files on that path are taken into account to determine the values of properties which are already used.
227→     *
228→     * @return Kotlin section editorconfig content. For example:
229→     * ```properties
230→     * final-newline=true
231→     * indent-size=4
232→     * ```
233→     */
234→    public fun generateKotlinEditorConfigSection(filePath: Path): String {
235→        val codeStyle =
236→            editorConfigOverride
237→                .properties[CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY]
238→                ?.parsed
239→                ?.safeAs<CodeStyleValue>()
240→                ?: CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY.defaultValue
241→        val rules =
242→            ruleProviders
243→                .map { it.createNewRuleInstance() }
244→                .distinctBy { it.ruleId }
245→                .toSet()
246→        return EditorConfigGenerator(fileSystem, editorConfigLoaderEc4j)
247→            .generateEditorconfig(
248→                rules,
249→                codeStyle,
250→                filePath,
251→            )
252→    }
253→
254→    /**
255→     * Reduce memory usage by cleaning internal caches.
256→     */
257→    public fun trimMemory() {
258→        THREAD_SAFE_EDITOR_CONFIG_CACHE.clear()
259→    }
260→
261→    /**
262→     * Get the list of files which will be accessed by KtLint when linting or formatting the given file or directory.
263→     * The API consumer can use this list to observe changes in '.editorconfig' files. Whenever such a change is
264→     * observed, the API consumer should call [reloadEditorConfigFile].
265→     * To avoid unnecessary access to the file system, it is best to call this method only once for the root of the
266→     * project which is to be [lint] or [format].
267→     */
268→    public fun editorConfigFilePaths(path: Path): List<Path> = EditorConfigFinder(editorConfigLoaderEc4j).findEditorConfigs(path)
269→
270→    /**
271→     * Reloads an '.editorconfig' file given that it is currently loaded into the KtLint cache. This method is intended
272→     * to be called by the API consumer when it is aware of changes in the '.editorconfig' file that should be taken
273→     * into account with next calls to [lint] and/or [format]. See [editorConfigFilePaths] to get the list of
274→     * '.editorconfig' files which need to be observed.
275→     */
276→    public fun reloadEditorConfigFile(path: Path) {
277→        THREAD_SAFE_EDITOR_CONFIG_CACHE.reloadIfExists(
278→            Resource.Resources.ofPath(path, StandardCharsets.UTF_8),
279→        )
280→    }
281→
282→    public fun transformToAst(code: Code): FileASTNode = createRuleExecutionContext(this, code).rootNode
283→
284→    public companion object {
285→        internal const val UTF8_BOM = "\uFEFF"
286→
287→        public const val STDIN_FILE: String = "<stdin>"
288→    }
289→}

0→# Changelog
 1→All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.
 2→This project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/).
 3→
 4→## Unreleased
 5→
 6→### Custom Rule Providers need to prepare for Kotlin 1.9
 7→
 8→In Kotlin 1.9 the extension points of the embedded kotlin compiler will change. Ktlint only uses the `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` extension point. This extension is not yet supported in 1.9, neither is it documented ([#KT-58704](https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-58704/Support-and-document-extension-point-org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler)). Without this extension point it might happen that your custom rules will throw exceptions during runtime. See [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981).
 9→
10→In Ktlint, 7 out of 77 rules needed small and sometimes bigger changes to become independent of the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler`. The impact on your custom rules may vary dependent on the way the autocorrect has been implemented. When manipulating `ASTNode`s there seems to be no impact. When, manipulating `PsiElement`s, some functions consistently result in a runtime exception.
11→
12→Based on the refactoring of the rules as provided by `ktlint-ruleset-standard` in Ktlint `0.49.x` the suggested refactoring is as follows:
13→
14→* Replace `LeafElement.replaceWithText(String)` with `LeafElement.rawReplaceWithText(String)`.
15→* Replace `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)` with `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)`. Note that this method inserts the new node (first) argument *before* the second argument node and as of that is not a simple replacement of the `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)`.
16→* Replace `PsiElement.replace(PsiElement)` with a sequence of `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)` and `AstNode.removeChild(AstNode)`.
17→
18→Be aware that your custom rules might use other functions which also throw exceptions when the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is no longer supported.
19→
20→In order to help you to analyse and fix the problems with your custom rules, ktlint temporarily supports to disable the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` using a flag. This flag is available in the Ktlint CLI and in the `KtlintRuleEngine`. By default, the extension point is enabled like it was in previous versions of ktlint.
21→
22→At least you should analyse the problems by running your test suits by running ktlint and disabling the extension point. Next you can start with fixing and releasing the updated rules. All rules in this version of ktlint have already been refactored and are not dependent on the extension point anymore. In Ktlint CLI the flag is to be activated with parameter `--disable-kotlin-extension-point`. API Consumers that use the `KtlintRuleEngine` directly, have to set property `enableKotlinCompilerExtensionPoint` to `false`.
23→
24→At this point in time, it is not yet decided what the next steps will be. Ktlint might drop the support of the extension points entirely. Or, if the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is fully supported at the time that ktlint will be based on kotlin 1.9 it might be kept. In either case, the flag will be dropped in a next version of ktlint.
25→
26→### Added
27→
28→* Add new experimental rule `binary-expression-wrapping`. This rule wraps a binary expression in case the max line length is exceeded ([#1940](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1940))
29→* Add flag to disable extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` to analyse impact on custom rules [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981)
30→* Add new experimental rule `no-empty-file` for all code styles. A kotlin (script) file may not be empty ([#1074](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1074))
31→* Add new experimental rule `statement-wrapping` which ensures function, class, or other blocks statement body doesn't start or end at starting or ending braces of the block ([#1938](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1938))
32→* Add new experimental rule `blank-line-before-declaration`. This rule requires a blank line before class, function or property declarations ([#1939](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1939))
33→
34→### Removed
35→
36→### Fixed
37→
38→* Do not flag a (potential) mutable extension property in case the getter is annotated or prefixed with a modifier `property-naming` ([#2024](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2024))
39→* Do not merge an annotated expression body with the function signature even if it fits on a single line ([#2043](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2043))
40→* Ignore property with name `serialVersionUID` in `property-naming` ([#2045](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2045))
41→* Prevent incorrect reporting of violations in case a nullable function type reference exceeds the maximum line length `parameter-list-wrapping` ([#1324](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1324)) 
42→* Prevent false negative on `else` branch when body contains only chained calls or binary expression ([#2057](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2057))
43→
44→### Changed
45→
46→* Fix Java interoperability issues with `RuleId` and `RuleSetId` classes. Those classes were defined as value classes in `0.49.0` and `0.49.1`. Although the classes were marked with `@JvmInline` it seems that it is not possible to uses those classes from Java base API Consumers like Spotless. The classes have now been replaced with data classes ([#2041](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2041))
47→* Update dependency `info.picocli:picocli` to v4.7.4
48→* Update dependency `org.junit.jupiter:junit-jupiter` to v5.9.3
49→* Update Kotlin development version to `1.8.22` and Kotlin version to `1.8.22`.
50→* Update dependency io.github.detekt.sarif4k:sarif4k to v0.4.0
51→* Update dependency org.jetbrains.dokka:dokka-gradle-plugin to v1.8.20
52→
53→## [0.49.1] - 2023-05-12
54→
55→### Added
56→
57→### Removed
58→
59→### Fixed
60→* Store path of file containing a lint violation relative to the location of the baseline file itself ([#1962](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1962))
61→* Print absolute path of file in lint violations when flag "--relative" is not specified in Ktlint CLI ([#1963](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1963)) 
62→* Handle parameter `--code-style=android_studio` in Ktlint CLI identical to deprecated parameter `--android` ([#1982](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1982))
63→* Prevent nullpointer exception (NPE) if class without body is followed by multiple blank lines until end of file `no-consecutive-blank-lines` ([#1987](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1987))
64→* Allow to 'unset' the `.editorconfig` property `ktlint_function_signature_rule_force_multiline_when_parameter_count_greater_or_equal_than` when using `ktlint_official` code style `function-signature` ([#1977](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1977))
65→* Prevent nullpointer exception (NPE) if or operator at start of line is followed by dot qualified expression `indent` ([#1993](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1993))
66→* Fix indentation of multiline parameter list in function literal `indent` ([#1976](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1976))
67→* Restrict indentation of closing quotes to `ktlint_official` code style to keep formatting of other code styles consistent with `0.48.x` and before `indent` ([#1971](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1971))
68→* Extract rule `no-single-line-block-comment` from `comment-wrapping` rule. The `no-single-line-block-comment` rule is added as experimental rule to the `ktlint_official` code style, but it can be enabled explicitly for the other code styles as well. ([#1980](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1980))
69→* Clean-up unwanted logging dependencies ([#1998](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1998))
70→* Fix directory traversal for patterns referring to paths outside of current working directory or any of it child directories ([#2002](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2002))
71→* Prevent multiple expressions on same line separated by semicolon ([#1078](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1078))
72→
73→### Changed
74→
75→* Moved class `Baseline` from `ktlint-cli` to `ktlint-cli-reporter-baseline` so that Baseline functionality is reusable for API Consumers.
76→
77→## [0.49.0] - 2023-04-21
78→
79→WARNING: This version of KtLint contains a number of breaking changes in KtLint CLI and KtLint API. If you are using KtLint with custom ruleset jars or custom reporter jars, then those need to be upgraded before you can use them with this version of ktlint. Please contact the maintainers of those jars and ask them to upgrade a.s.a.p.
80→
81→All rule id's in the output of Ktlint are now prefixed with a rule set. Although KtLint currently supports standard rules to be unqualified, users are encouraged to include the rule set id in all references to rules. This includes following:
82→* The `--disabled-rules` parameter in KtLint CLI.
83→* The `.editorconfig` properties used to enable or disable rule and rule sets. Note that properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` have been removed in this release. See [disabled rules](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/configuration-ktlint/#disabled-rules) for more information.
84→* The `source` element in the KtLint CLI `baseline.xml` file. Regenerating this file, fixes all rule references automatically.
85→* The KtLint disable directives `ktlint-enable` / `ktlint-disable` and the `@Suppress('ktlint:...')` annotations.
86→* The `VisitorModifier.RunAfterRule`.
87→
88→### Moving experimental rules to standard rule set
89→
90→The `experimental` rule set has been merged with `standard` rule set. The rules which formerly were part of the `experimental` rule set are still being treated as before. The rules will only be run in case `.editorconfig` property `ktlint_experimental` is enabled or in case the rule is explicitly enabled.
91→
92→Note that the prefix `experimental:` has to be removed from all references to this rule. Check references in:
93→* The `--disabled-rules` parameter in KtLint CLI.
94→* The `.editorconfig` properties used to enable or disable rule and rule sets. Note that properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` have been removed in this release. See [disabled rules](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/configuration-ktlint/#disabled-rules) for more information.
95→* The KtLint disable directives `ktlint-enable` / `ktlint-disable` and the `@Suppress('ktlint:...')` annotations.
96→* The `VisitorModifier.RunAfterRule`.
97→
98→### Promote experimental rules to non-experimental
99→

1→package com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api
 2→
 3→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.KtLintRuleEngine.Companion.STDIN_FILE
 4→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.konan.file.file
 5→import java.io.File
 6→import java.nio.file.Path
 7→import kotlin.io.path.pathString
 8→
 9→/**
10→ * A representation of a block of code. Use one of the factory methods [fromFile], [fromPath], [fromSnippet] or [fromStdin] to instantiate.
11→ */
12→public class Code private constructor(
13→    public val content: String,
14→    public val fileName: String?,
15→    public val filePath: Path?,
16→    public val script: Boolean,
17→    public val isStdIn: Boolean,
18→) {
19→    public fun fileNameOrStdin(): String =
20→        if (isStdIn) {
21→            STDIN_FILE
22→        } else {
23→            fileName.orEmpty()
24→        }
25→
26→    public companion object {
27→        /**
28→         * Create [Code] from a [file] containing valid Kotlin code or script. The '.editorconfig' files on the path to [file] are taken
29→         * into account.
30→         */
31→        public fun fromFile(file: File): Code =
32→            Code(
33→                content = file.readText(),
34→                fileName = file.name,
35→                filePath = file.toPath(),
36→                script = file.name.endsWith(".kts", ignoreCase = true),
37→                isStdIn = false,
38→            )
39→
40→        /**
41→         * Create [Code] from a [path] to a file containing valid Kotlin code or script. The '.editorconfig' files on the path to [file] are
42→         * taken into account. This method is intended to be used in unit tests. In order to work with the Ktlint test file system it needs
43→         * to make additional call to get the file system which makes it slower compared to [fromFile]. Prefer to use [fromFile].
44→         */
45→        public fun fromPath(path: Path): Code {
46→            // Resolve the file based on the file system of the original path given.
47→            val file =
48→                path
49→                    .fileSystem
50→                    .file(path.pathString)
51→            return Code(
52→                content = file.readStrings().joinToString(separator = "\n"),
53→                fileName = file.name,
54→                filePath = path,
55→                script = file.name.endsWith(".kts", ignoreCase = true),
56→                isStdIn = false,
57→            )
58→        }
59→
60→        /**
61→         * The [content] represent a valid piece of Kotlin code or Kotlin script. The '.editorconfig' files on the filesystem are ignored as
62→         * the snippet is not associated with a file path. Use [Code.fromFile] for scanning a file while at the same time respecting the
63→         * '.editorconfig' files on the path to the file.
64→         */
65→        public fun fromSnippet(
66→            content: String,
67→            script: Boolean = false,
68→        ): Code =
69→            Code(
70→                content = content,
71→                filePath = null,
72→                fileName = null,
73→                script = script,
74→                isStdIn = true,
75→            )
76→
77→        /**
78→         * Create [Code] by reading the snippet from 'stdin'. No '.editorconfig' are taken into account.  The '.editorconfig' files on the
79→         * filesystem are ignored as the snippet is not associated with a file path. Use [Code.fromFile] for scanning a file while at the
80→         * same time respecting the '.editorconfig' files on the path to the file.
81→         */
82→        public fun fromStdin(): Code = fromSnippet(String(System.`in`.readBytes()))
83→    }
84→}

 97→    public fun lint(
 98→        code: Code,
 99→        callback: (LintError) -> Unit = { },
100→    ) {
101→        LOGGER.debug { "Starting with linting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'" }
102→
103→        val ruleExecutionContext = createRuleExecutionContext(this, code)
104→        val errors = mutableListOf<LintError>()
105→
106→        VisitorProvider(ruleExecutionContext.ruleProviders)
107→            .visitor()
108→            .invoke { rule ->
109→                ruleExecutionContext.executeRule(rule, false) { offset, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected ->
110→                    val (line, col) = ruleExecutionContext.positionInTextLocator(offset)
111→                    errors.add(LintError(line, col, rule.ruleId, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected))
112→                }
113→            }
114→
115→        errors
116→            .sortedWith { l, r -> if (l.line != r.line) l.line - r.line else l.col - r.col }
117→            .forEach { e -> callback(e) }
118→
119→        LOGGER.debug { "Finished with linting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'" }
120→    }

122→    /**
123→     * Fix style violations in [code] for lint errors when possible. If [code] is passed as file reference then the '.editorconfig' files on
124→     * the path are taken into account. For each lint violation found, the [callback] is invoked.
125→     *
126→     * @throws KtLintParseException if text is not a valid Kotlin code
127→     * @throws KtLintRuleException in case of internal failure caused by a bug in rule implementation
128→     */
129→    public fun format(
130→        code: Code,
131→        callback: (LintError, Boolean) -> Unit = { _, _ -> },
132→    ): String {
133→        LOGGER.debug { "Starting with formatting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'" }
134→
135→        val hasUTF8BOM = code.content.startsWith(UTF8_BOM)
136→        val ruleExecutionContext = createRuleExecutionContext(this, code)
137→
138→        var tripped = false
139→        var mutated = false
140→        val errors = mutableSetOf<Pair<LintError, Boolean>>()
141→        val visitorProvider = VisitorProvider(ruleExecutionContext.ruleProviders)
142→        visitorProvider
143→            .visitor()
144→            .invoke { rule ->
145→                ruleExecutionContext.executeRule(rule, true) { offset, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected ->
146→                    tripped = true
147→                    if (canBeAutoCorrected) {
148→                        mutated = true
149→                        /**
150→                         * Rebuild the suppression locator after each change in the AST as the offsets of the
151→                         * suppression hints might have changed.
152→                         */
153→                        ruleExecutionContext.rebuildSuppressionLocator()
154→                    }
155→                    val (line, col) = ruleExecutionContext.positionInTextLocator(offset)
156→                    errors.add(
157→                        Pair(
158→                            LintError(line, col, rule.ruleId, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected),
159→                            // It is assumed that a rule that emits that an error can be autocorrected, also
160→                            // does correct the error.
161→                            canBeAutoCorrected,
162→                        ),
163→                    )
164→                    // In trace mode report the violation immediately. The order in which violations are actually found might be different
165→                    // from the order in which they are reported. For debugging purposes it cn be helpful to know the exact order in which
166→                    // violations are being solved.
167→                    LOGGER.trace {
168→                        "Format violation: ${code.fileNameOrStdin()}:$line:$col: $errorMessage (${rule.ruleId})" +
169→                            canBeAutoCorrected.ifFalse { " [cannot be autocorrected]" }
170→                    }
171→                }
172→            }
173→        if (tripped) {
174→            visitorProvider
175→                .visitor()
176→                .invoke { rule ->
177→                    ruleExecutionContext.executeRule(
178→                        rule,
179→                        false,
180→                    ) { offset, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected ->
181→                        val (line, col) = ruleExecutionContext.positionInTextLocator(offset)
182→                        errors.add(
183→                            Pair(
184→                                LintError(line, col, rule.ruleId, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected),
185→                                // It is assumed that a rule only corrects an error after it has emitted an
186→                                // error and indicating that it actually can be autocorrected.
187→                                false,
188→                            ),
189→                        )
190→                    }
191→                }
192→        }
193→
194→        errors
195→            .sortedWith { (l), (r) -> if (l.line != r.line) l.line - r.line else l.col - r.col }
196→            .forEach { (e, corrected) -> callback(e, corrected) }
197→
198→        if (!mutated) {
199→            return code.content
200→        }
201→
202→        val formattedCode =
203→            ruleExecutionContext
204→                .rootNode
205→                .text
206→                .replace("\n", ruleExecutionContext.determineLineSeparator(code.content))
207→        return if (hasUTF8BOM) {
208→            UTF8_BOM + formattedCode
209→        } else {
210→            formattedCode
211→        }.also {
212→            LOGGER.debug("Finished with formatting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'")
213→        }
214→    }

216→    private fun RuleExecutionContext.determineLineSeparator(fileContent: String): String {
217→        val eol = editorConfig[END_OF_LINE_PROPERTY]
218→        return when {
219→            eol == crlf || eol != lf && fileContent.lastIndexOf('\r') != -1 -> "\r\n"
220→            else -> "\n"
221→        }
222→    }

0→# Changelog
 1→All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.

 4→## Unreleased
 5→
 6→### Custom Rule Providers need to prepare for Kotlin 1.9
 7→
 8→In Kotlin 1.9 the extension points of the embedded kotlin compiler will change. Ktlint only uses the `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` extension point. This extension is not yet supported in 1.9, neither is it documented ([#KT-58704](https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-58704/Support-and-document-extension-point-org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler)). Without this extension point it might happen that your custom rules will throw exceptions during runtime. See [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981).
 9→
10→In Ktlint, 7 out of 77 rules needed small and sometimes bigger changes to become independent of the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler`. The impact on your custom rules may vary dependent on the way the autocorrect has been implemented. When manipulating `ASTNode`s there seems to be no impact. When, manipulating `PsiElement`s, some functions consistently result in a runtime exception.
11→
12→Based on the refactoring of the rules as provided by `ktlint-ruleset-standard` in Ktlint `0.49.x` the suggested refactoring is as follows:
13→
14→* Replace `LeafElement.replaceWithText(String)` with `LeafElement.rawReplaceWithText(String)`.
15→* Replace `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)` with `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)`. Note that this method inserts the new node (first) argument *before* the second argument node and as of that is not a simple replacement of the `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)`.
16→* Replace `PsiElement.replace(PsiElement)` with a sequence of `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)` and `AstNode.removeChild(AstNode)`.
17→
18→Be aware that your custom rules might use other functions which also throw exceptions when the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is no longer supported.
19→
20→In order to help you to analyse and fix the problems with your custom rules, ktlint temporarily supports to disable the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` using a flag. This flag is available in the Ktlint CLI and in the `KtlintRuleEngine`. By default, the extension point is enabled like it was in previous versions of ktlint.
21→
22→At least you should analyse the problems by running your test suits by running ktlint and disabling the extension point. Next you can start with fixing and releasing the updated rules. All rules in this version of ktlint have already been refactored and are not dependent on the extension point anymore. In Ktlint CLI the flag is to be activated with parameter `--disable-kotlin-extension-point`. API Consumers that use the `KtlintRuleEngine` directly, have to set property `enableKotlinCompilerExtensionPoint` to `false`.
23→
24→At this point in time, it is not yet decided what the next steps will be. Ktlint might drop the support of the extension points entirely. Or, if the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is fully supported at the time that ktlint will be based on kotlin 1.9 it might be kept. In either case, the flag will be dropped in a next version of ktlint.
25→
26→### Added
27→
28→* Add new experimental rule `binary-expression-wrapping`. This rule wraps a binary expression in case the max line length is exceeded ([#1940](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1940))
29→* Add flag to disable extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` to analyse impact on custom rules [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981)
30→* Add new experimental rule `no-empty-file` for all code styles. A kotlin (script) file may not be empty ([#1074](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1074))
31→* Add new experimental rule `statement-wrapping` which ensures function, class, or other blocks statement body doesn't start or end at starting or ending braces of the block ([#1938](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1938))
32→* Add new experimental rule `blank-line-before-declaration`. This rule requires a blank line before class, function or property declarations ([#1939](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1939))

37→
38→* Do not flag a (potential) mutable extension property in case the getter is annotated or prefixed with a modifier `property-naming` ([#2024](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2024))
39→* Do not merge an annotated expression body with the function signature even if it fits on a single line ([#2043](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2043))

Although the classes were marked with `@JvmInline` it seems that it is not possible to uses those classes from Java base API Consumers like Spotless. The classes have now been replaced with data classes ([#2041](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2041))

77→## [0.49.0] - 2023-04-21

79→WARNING: This version of KtLint contains a number of breaking changes in KtLint CLI and KtLint API. If you are using KtLint with custom ruleset jars or custom reporter jars, then those need to be upgraded before you can use them with this version of ktlint. Please contact the maintainers of those jars and ask them to upgrade a.s.a.p.

81→All rule id's in the output of Ktlint are now prefixed with a rule set. Although KtLint currently supports standard rules to be unqualified, users are encouraged to include the rule set id in all references to rules. This includes following:
82→* The `--disabled-rules` parameter in KtLint CLI.
83→* The `.editorconfig` properties used to enable or disable rule and rule sets. Note that properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` have been removed in this release. See [disabled rules](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/configuration-ktlint/#disabled-rules) for more information.
84→* The `source` element in the KtLint CLI `baseline.xml` file. Regenerating this file, fixes all rule references automatically.
85→* The KtLint disable directives `ktlint-enable` / `ktlint-disable` and the `@Suppress('ktlint:...')` annotations.
86→* The `VisitorModifier.RunAfterRule`.
